risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After written consent by the parents and assent by the children, the subjects were randomized into an exercise or a control group. The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded." Comment: The study explicitly states that participants were randomized, and the article adds context that the allocation process was blinded and that the randomization procedure had been described in detail elsewhere. Although the exact random method is not reproduced here, the wording supports a genuine random allocation sequence rather than a predictable rule.

_evidence.method_quote: The study is the second part of a randomized controlled trial of 54 children and adolescents with JIA that studied the effects of an exercise programme on bone health, muscle strength, fitness and well-being. Bone health and leisure time activities have been reported earlier and the randomization process has been described in detail [23]. ... After written consent by the parents and assent by the children, the subjects were randomized into an exercise or a control group. The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomized into exercise or control group; the paper says the randomization process was described elsewhere.
context_clues: randomization process has been described in detail
randomized into an exercise or a control group

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded." Comment: This indicates the allocator was blinded, but the paper does not explicitly describe a concealment mechanism that would prevent foreknowledge of the next assignment (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, or identical numbered containers). The extracted evidence says the mechanism was "not described," so concealment cannot be confirmed as low risk; there is also no positive evidence of a predictable/open allocation procedure, so high risk is not justified.

_evidence.concealment_quote: The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded.
_evidence.note: Article states the randomization process was described elsewhere; this paper only says the allocator was blinded and does not describe the concealment method.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "There were 10 dropouts from the control group after the randomization, as they had preferred to belong to the exercise group." Comment: This indicates participants knew which group they were in and that knowledge affected behavior/preferences. The study does not describe any blinding of participants or intervention-delivering personnel, while several outcomes were subjective/patient-reported (e.g. pain VAS, CHAQ/CHQ, Borg scale), so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence the outcomes overall.

_evidence.blinding_quote: The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded. ... The same physiotherapist, who was blinded to the previous measurement, performed all measurements.
_evidence.blinding_method: The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded; measurements were performed by a physiotherapist blinded to previous measurement. No participant/personnel blinding method described.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The same physiotherapist, who was blinded to the previous measurement, performed all measurements." Comment: The outcome assessor was a physiotherapist and was blinded to previous measurement, but not clearly to treatment allocation. More importantly, the study included patient-reported outcomes (e.g. CHAQ, CHQ, Borg scale, pain), and the patients were not reported as blinded (participants_blinded: unclear). Because self-reported subjective outcomes are vulnerable to detection bias when participants know allocation, and the worst-outcome rule applies, the domain is High risk.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: The same physiotherapist, who was blinded to the previous measurement, performed all measurements.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "There were 10 dropouts from the control group after the randomization, as they had preferred to belong to the exercise group. There were another six dropouts after the first test occasion due to the families’ lack of time." Comment: For the analysed repeated-measures strength outcome, the paper reports exercise group n=33 randomised and n=26 analysed (7 lost) versus control group n=21 randomised and n=19 analysed (2 lost), giving differential attrition of 13.4 percentage points. The reasons include post-randomization preference for the other arm and lack of time, and there is no reported missing-data method to mitigate this imbalance, so attrition could bias the outcome.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “There were another six dropouts after the first test occasion due to the families’ lack of time.”; later: “The participants in the exercise group fulfilled about 70% of expected numbers of exercises”

“There were 10 dropouts from the control group after the randomization, as they had preferred to belong to the exercise group.”; “There were another six dropouts after the first test occasion due to the families’ lack of time.”

_evidence.notes: Per-arm analysed numbers come from Table 4 (exercise n=26, control n=19) for the repeated-measures strength outcomes; other outcomes have different analysed Ns (e.g., Table 6 exercise n=25, control n=17; Table 7 varies by outcome).
